title: Morphological and metabolomics profiling of intraspecific Arabidopsis hybrids in relation to biomass heterosis.
author_string: Le, Quynh Thi Ngoc, Sugi, Naoya, Yamaguchi, Masaaki, Hirayama, Touko, Kobayashi, Makoto et al.
abstract: Heterosis contributes greatly to the worldwide agricultural yield. However, the molecular mechanism underlying heterosis remains unclear. This study took advantage of Arabidopsis intraspecific hybrids to identify heterosis-related metabolites. Forty-six intraspecific hybrids were used to examine parental effects on seed area and germination time. The degree of heterosis was evaluated based on biomass: combinations showing high heterosis of F<sub>1</sub> hybrids exhibited a biomass increase from 6.1 to 44% over the better parent value (BPV), whereas that of the low- and no-heterosis hybrids ranged from - 19.8 to 9.8% over the BPV. Metabolomics analyses of F<sub>1</sub> hybrids with high heterosis and those with low one suggested that changes in TCA cycle intermediates are key factors that control growth. Notably, higher fumarate/malate ratios were observed in the high heterosis F<sub>1</sub> hybrids, suggesting they provide metabolic support associated with the increased biomass. These hybrids may produce more energy-intensive biomass by speeding up the efficiency of TCA fluxes. However, the expression levels of TCA-process-related genes in F<sub>1</sub> hybrids were not associated with the intensity of heterosis, suggesting that the post-transcriptional or post-translational regulation of these genes may affect the productivity of the intermediates in the TCA cycle.
